  "proof_summaries": [
    {
      "exhaustive_search_summary": "Searched 1850 census (FamilySearch, Ancestry, MyHeritage — log_001, log_002, log_003), 1860 census (FamilySearch — log_004), and death certificate (FamilySearch — log_005). Probate search in progress. 1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses not yet searched.",
      "id": "ps_001",
      "narrative_markdown": "## Parentage of Patrick Flynn (ca. 1845–1908)\n\nPatrick Flynn is **Probably** the son of Thomas Flynn of Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania.\n\n### Evidence Summary\n\nThree independent lines of evidence support this conclusion:\n\n1. **1850 U.S. Census** (Original Source). Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland, appears in the household of Thomas Flynn, age 32, born Ireland, in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania (dwelling 84, family 91). The 1850 census does not state relationships, but Patrick's position in the household and shared surname are consistent with a parent-child relationship. The informant is indeterminate but likely a household member. This constitutes indirect evidence of parentage.\n\n2. **1860 U.S. Census** (Original Source). Patrick Flynn, age 15, born Ireland, appears in the household of Thomas Flynn in Schuylkill County (dwelling 112, family 119). Like the 1850 census, the 1860 census does not state relationships explicitly (the relationship column was not introduced until 1880). Patrick's position in the household, shared surname, and age consistent with the 1850 enumeration support a parent-child relationship. This constitutes indirect evidence of parentage.\n\n3. **1908 Death Certificate** (Original Source). Patrick Flynn's death certificate (no. 4521, Pennsylvania Department of Health) names \"Thomas Flynn\" as his father. The informant was James Brown, identified as Patrick's son-in-law. As a son-in-law reporting his father-in-law's parentage, Brown is a secondary informant for this fact — he was not a witness to Patrick's birth and is reporting what he was told. Nevertheless, this is direct evidence naming the father.\n\n### Conflict Resolution\n\nA birthplace conflict exists: the 1850 and 1860 censuses list Patrick's birthplace as Ireland, while the 1908 death certificate states Pennsylvania. The two census records are contemporary recordings with informants likely present in the household, while the death certificate was created 63 years after Patrick's birth by a son-in-law with no firsthand knowledge of the event. Per the GPS preponderance hierarchy, contemporary recordings by closer informants outweigh later recollections by secondary informants. Ireland is accepted as the birthplace; the death certificate entry is attributed to informant error.\n\n### Assessment\n\nThe conclusion is rated **Probable** rather than **Proved** because: (a) both census sources (1850 and 1860) provide only indirect evidence of parentage (relationships not stated — the relationship column was not introduced until 1880), (b) the death certificate informant is secondary, and (c) research is not yet exhaustive — the 1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses have not been searched, and Thomas Flynn's probate records have not been located. If Thomas Flynn's will names Patrick as a son, or if additional census records confirm the relationship, the conclusion would advance to **Proved**.\n\n### Citations\n\n1. 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 84, family 91, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 1 May 2026.\n2. 1860 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 112, family 119, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M653, roll 1141; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026.\n3. Pennsylvania Department of Health, death certificate no. 4521 (1908), Patrick Flynn; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 3 May 2026.",

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy

The tree edit successfully created Ruth (I5) and Henry (I6) as new persons with proper parent-child relationships to George (I1) and Martha (I2). No fabricated material or unsupported claims.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all required work: extracted all six household members' facts from the census record, created a source entry, wrote 40 assertions covering name, age, birth year, birthplace, occupation (for head), residence, and relationships for each person. The critical household-children dedup was performed via a single add_household_children call that correctly matched the in-tree parents and skipped Anna/Samuel while creating Ruth/Henry. All items the input state required were completed.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All five MCP tool calls passed correct arguments. project_context calls were appropriate for retrieving state. research_log_append correctly documented the user-provided record with proper metadata. research_append correctly structured 40 assertion entries with all required fields (record_id, record_role, fact_type, value, evidence_type, informant, informant_proximity, etc.). tree_edit add_household_children call correctly passed both parents (George and Martha) and all four children (Anna, Samuel, Ruth, Henry) by name/gender, enabling proper dedup. No fixture_not_found errors; all calls matched expected patterns.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact. Compound information was properly decomposed: name, age, birth year (computed), birthplace, occupation, and residence are separate assertions. Relationships (spouse, child-of-parent) are separate. Date and place attributes of a single event (e.g., birth date and birthplace) are correctly held in separate assertions with their own fact_type, not mixed into one value field. No compound claims crammed into single assertions.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informants are correctly identified and proximity is properly assessed. For census household-reported facts (name, age, birthplace, occupation), informant is 'unknown household member' with proximity 'household_member'—correctly distinguishing the household member who reported the information from the enumerator who recorded it. For residence, informant is 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness'—correct doctrine for census residence (enumerator personally observed the dwelling). All informant_proximity values are from the closed enum and defensible.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned. Census-stated facts (name, age, birthplace, occupation, residence) are marked 'direct'—the census explicitly states these in its columns. Computed birth years are marked 'indirect'—they are inferred from stated age minus census year, not stated directly. This correctly applies the stated-vs-inferred doctrine without conflating 'the informant didn't witness the birth' (an information_quality concern) with evidence_type. The two birth assertions per person (one for place/direct, one for date/indirect) are correct by design.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All extracted facts are accurate and supported by the marriage record. Names, ages, birthplaces, occupations, parents, and marriage details are correctly transcribed. Birth years (~1845, ~1848) are correctly computed from stated ages. Informant identifications are accurate: groom and bride as self-informants for their own facts, Rev. O'Connor as officiant for the marriage event. No fabricated material or unsupported claims.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All required information from the marriage record was extracted: both groom and bride with full biographical details (name, age, birthplace, residence, occupation, parents), marriage event details (date, place, officiant), and witnesses identified. The skill created 23 assertions across 7 persons, including stub entries for parents and witnesses. All data present in the source was addressed.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill made 7 MCP tool calls. The first 4 calls (project_context, research_log_append, project_context, research_append) were correct. However, the 5th call (tree_edit with add_person operations) failed validation with schema errors (missing 'given'/'surname' fields, unexpected 'nameForms' property). The skill immediately recovered with a 6th corrected call using the proper schema (given/surname directly on the name object), which succeeded. This single clean recovery from a validation error scores partial rather than full credit because the initial call showed a schema misunderstanding, though the recovery was competent and the final persisted state is correct.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion represents a single extractable fact. Compound information is properly decomposed: name, age, birthplace (place), birth year (date), residence, occupation, and relationships are separate assertions. Marriage events carry both date and place fields as designed (one event with two attributes), not compound facts. No assertions mix multiple distinct facts into a single value field.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informants are correctly identified and proximity is accurate. For Patrick and Catherine's biographical facts (name, age, birthplace, residence, occupation, parents), informant is the party themselves with proximity 'self' — they provided this information to the clerk. For the marriage event (date, place), informant is Rev. Daniel O'Connor (officiant) with proximity 'official_duty' — correct doctrine for a marriage record. Witnesses (Thomas Flynn, James Gallagher) are identified by name with informant_proximity 'official_duty' as recorded by the officiant/clerk. aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned. Now I'll delegate to the record-extractor agent.Extraction is complete. Here's the summary:

---

**Source created:** `src_001` — *Schuylkill County, PA Marriage License & Certificate No. 4872* (capture provenance)

**23 assertions written across 7 persons:**

| Person | Tree ID | Role | Assertions |
|--------|---------|------|-----------|
| Patrick Flynn | I1 (existing) | Groom | Name, age, birth ~1845, birthplace Ireland, residence, occupation (coal miner), child of Thomas Flynn & Mary Brennan, marriage |
| Catherine Gallagher | I2 (new) | Bride | Name, age, birth ~1848, birthplace Pennsylvania, residence, child of James Gallagher & Bridget Dolan, marriage |
| Thomas Flynn | I3 (stub) | Groom's father / Witness | Name |
| Mary Brennan | I4 (stub) | Groom's mother | Name |
| James Gallagher | I5 (stub) | Bride's father / Witness | Name |
| Bridget Dolan | I6 (stub) | Bride's mother | Name |

**5 relationships added:** couple (I1–I2), two parent-child pairs for Patrick, two for Catherine.

**Notable flags:**
- ⚠️ **Place geocoding misfired** on "Shenandoah, Schuylkill County, PA" (resolved to New Zealand) and the Church of the Annunciation marriage place (resolved to Michigan). These `standard_place` values need manual correction via `tree-edit` before any mapping work.
- ✅ Witness dual-role confirmed: Thomas Flynn = groom's father (I3); James Gallagher = bride's father (I5) — corroborative detail noted in bias notes.

**Recommended next steps:** run `check-warnings` on the new persons, correct the misfired place values, then `person-evidence` to formally link the personae to I1 and I2.

---

## Extraction Complete — `src_001` (S1)

**27 assertions written** (`a_001`–`a_027`) across 11 persons.

### Assertions by person

| Person | ID | Key assertions |
|---|---|---|
| Harold Dean Whitaker | I2 | Name, birth (8 Jan 1930, Brigham City UT), death (14 Mar 2021, Logan UT), age (91), residence, parentage, marriage, sibling |
| Walter Whitaker (father) | I3 | Name, preceded in death |
| Edith Whitaker (mother) | I4 | Name, preceded in death |
| Ruth Ann Baxter (wife) | I5 | Name, preceded in death; marriage (3 Jun 1952, Logan LDS Temple) |
| Grace (Whitaker) Tolman (sister) | I6 | Name, residence (Ogden UT), sibling relationship |
| Robert Whitaker (son) | I7 | Name, residence (Portland OR), child relationship |
| Linda (spouse of Robert) | I8 | Name |
| Linda (Whitaker) Merrill (daughter) | I9 | Name, residence (Twin Falls ID), child relationship |
| Paul Merrill (son-in-law) | I10 | Name |
| Karen Foster (neighbor) | I11 | Name (FAN associate only — no familial relationship asserted) |

### Classifications (applied uniformly)
- **Source:** `authored` (obituary; original publication not examined)
- **Information:** `secondary` (family informant, family not present at events)
- **Evidence:** `direct` for all stated facts; `indirect` for the three "preceded in death" assertions (death inferred, no date/place given)

### Tree changes
- **10 new persons** created (I2–I11)
- **Relationships:** Walter & Edith → Harold (R1–R2); Walter & Edith → Grace (R3–R4, establishing sibling link); Harold–Ruth couple + marriage (R5); Harold/Ruth → Robert (R6–R7) and → Linda Merrill (R8–R9); Robert–Linda couple (R10); Linda–Paul Merrill couple (R11)

---

### ⚠ Flags to address

1. **Original not examined** — The underlying publication (newspaper or funeral home site) was never reached. All dates and places need corroboration from primary sources (birth certificate, death certificate, marriage record).
2. **Place geocoding misfired** — The geocoder resolved Utah/Idaho localities to European/UK places. The `standard_place` values for Logan UT, Brigham City UT, and Twin Falls ID need manual correction against US geographic authorities.
3. **No open research questions** — This project currently has no named questions; all `extracted_for_question_ids` are empty. Consider adding research questions (e.g., confirming birth, death, and marriage dates) to focus future work.

**Suggested next step:** Run `check-warnings` to review the new tree persons, then correct the misfired place geocodes.

entry.informant_bias_notes: Birth date and place supplied by presenting parent(s) at christening nine days after birth; parent has first-hand knowledge of birth facts. Record is a derivative index — original register image unexamined.

Index reads 'Nadnesen'; likely misread of 'Aadnesen' (son of Aadne, an attested Old Norse/Norwegian name — an indexer or OCR could drop the leading 'Aa' or misread it as 'N'). Given name Tollev is consistent with the child's user-supplied patronymic 'Tollefsen' (son of Tollev/Tollef). Original register image unreachable (image group 13729_012345 invalid). Confirm against original Stavanger kirkebøker image before treating surname as established.
Residence recorded in christening register; father's farm name 'Birkeland' aligns with the child's user-supplied surname 'Birkeland', supporting the identification.

Patronymic surname 'Larsdatter' is a derived form (daughter of Lars); secondary information in the sense that it encodes the maternal grandfather's given name, but the name itself as recorded is taken as stated.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified the suspect father-name transcription ('Nadnesen' as likely misread of 'Aadnesen'), recorded it tentatively with [?] marking and indeterminate information_quality, documented the reasoning in informant_bias_notes, and explicitly named original-image confirmation as the outstanding step. The child's christening facts (11/20 July 1817, Stavanger) and mother (Inger Larsdatter) were extracted accurately. No fabricated material; all claims are supported by the record_read response and the user's own flagged concern. The skill correctly declined to finalize the suspect required identifier as established.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all required elements: extracted the father (marked tentative), the child's christening and birth facts, the mother, and all three persons' assertions. The research log was appended documenting the image-unreachable outcome and the tentative-pending-confirmation decision. The source was recorded as derivative with notes flagging the unexamined original. The skill explicitly stated the next step (examine original Stavanger kirkebøker image via Digitalarkivet or FamilySearch film viewer) and provided actionable routes to obtain it.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: Most tool calls matched expected arguments correctly (record_read with correct ARK, research_log_append, research_append with proper assertion structure). However, the final tree_edit call to add_relationship passed parent/child IDs (I3, I2, I4) that were assigned by the prior tree_edit call, not pre-existing identifiers — this is correct idempotent behavior per the schema. The add_household_children call passed 'Nadnesen' (without [?]) in the parent surname, while the persisted assertion correctly has 'Nadnesen [?]' — this is a minor mismatch on a non-critical arg (the household-children tool skipped the child anyway due to no parent in tree), but the surname passed did not match the tentative-marked value in the assertions. This is a partial rather than full pass.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact. The child's christening is one assertion with date and place fields (correct schema design, not compound). Birth is separate from christening. The father's name is one assertion; residence is separate. The mother's name and residence are separate. Relationship assertions are atomic (one parent-child edge per assertion). No compound values mixing multiple facts into a single assertion value field.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informants are correctly identified: child's name/birth/christening attributed to 'presenting parent(s)' with proximity 'household_member' (the household member reported the facts to the enumerator/minister). Father's name attributed to 'unknown (index/transcription)' with proximity 'unknown' — appropriate because the index is the source and the original informant cannot be identified without reading the register. Father's residence attributed to 'presenting parent(s)' with proximity 'household_member'. Mother's name and residence similarly attributed. The skill correctly distinguished the recorder (index/minister) from the informant (household member who supplied the facts).

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: All evidence_type assignments are correct. The child's name, birth, and christening are marked 'direct' — the index states these facts explicitly. The father's name is marked 'direct' — the index states 'Tollev Nadnesen' explicitly (the doubt is about transcription accuracy, not whether it is stated, per the grading note). Father's residence is 'direct' — stated in the index. Mother's name and residence are 'direct' — stated. The [?] marking and indeterminate information_quality capture the transcription doubt at the appropriate layer (accuracy/quality), not by downgrading evidence_type to indirect. This correctly applies the doctrine that stated facts are direct even when their accuracy is questioned.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informant proximity values are defensible and correctly applied. Household members reporting on their own household at christening are 'household_member' (correct for census-like household-member reports). The father's name from the index is 'unknown' proximity (the original informant in the register cannot be identified without reading the image). The skill did not conflate the index/enumerator with the original household informant. All proximity values are from the closed enum and appropriately justified in the bias notes.
